content.text: What It Is

Scaled Agile Frameworks—SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework), LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum), and Scrum@Scale—are organisational operating models designed to coordinate multiple agile teams working towards a shared programme objective. When applied to agentic AI programmes, these frameworks provide the coordination structures—alignment rhythms, shared backlog management, dependency resolution, and release synchronisation—that prevent multiple AI development teams from drifting into incoherent, incompatible implementations despite working autonomously within their sprints.

SAFe is the most structured of the three frameworks, organising teams into Agile Release Trains (ARTs) that synchronise on a twelve-week Programme Increment (PI) cadence. Each PI begins with a two-day PI Planning event where all teams in the ART plan their next twelve weeks together, surface cross-team dependencies, and commit to a shared set of PI Objectives. LeSS takes a simpler approach, scaling Scrum directly without introducing additional coordination roles or artefacts beyond what standard Scrum requires, relying on joint Sprint Planning and end-of-Sprint review events to maintain alignment across teams. Scrum@Scale combines elements of both, providing a modular framework that organisations can configure to their specific scaling needs.

Applying any of these frameworks to agentic AI programmes requires adaptation. Standard scaled agile frameworks were designed for software product development with relatively predictable feature backlogs. Agentic AI work includes experimental tasks—model training experiments, evaluation design, red-teaming—that do not fit naturally into standard story-point-based sprint planning. The frameworks must be adapted to accommodate the exploratory nature of AI development while preserving the coordination benefits they provide.

A key decision when applying scaled agile frameworks to AI programmes is how to handle the AI-specific platform teams—the teams that maintain model serving infrastructure, evaluation harnesses, and shared data pipelines—in relation to the feature teams that build agent capabilities on top of those platforms. In SAFe, platform teams are typically part of a separate Platform ART. In LeSS, they may be component teams with explicit coordination obligations to the feature teams that depend on them. This structural decision significantly affects how dependencies are managed and how platform work is prioritised relative to feature delivery.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI programmes that scale to four or more teams without a shared coordination framework rapidly develop alignment problems. Teams build incompatible interfaces, duplicate platform work, make conflicting architectural decisions, and miss shared integration milestones because no mechanism exists to surface and resolve the conflicts before they become delivery blockers. Scaled agile frameworks provide the coordination structures that prevent these failures—not by eliminating team autonomy but by creating the shared rhythms and shared artefacts that enable autonomous teams to remain aligned.

The discipline of PI Planning, which requires all teams in an ART to surface cross-team dependencies explicitly and accept or negotiate them before committing to their PI Objectives, is particularly valuable for agentic AI programmes where platform dependencies—a team depending on the model serving team to deploy a new inference endpoint before they can complete their sprint—are common and consequential. Without a structured dependency identification event, these dependencies remain implicit until they become blockers.

Scaled agile frameworks also provide a cadence for incorporating governance and compliance activities. For agentic AI programmes in regulated industries, compliance reviews, security assessments, and ethical AI evaluations need to be integrated into the delivery cadence without disrupting team velocity. SAFe provides explicit roles—the System Architect, the Release Train Engineer, the Product Management function—that can own the governance integration points within the ART cadence.
Benefits
Dependency coordination:
Structured dependency identification at PI Planning or joint Sprint Planning prevents the most common source of multi-team integration failures.
Aligned release cadence:
Synchronised releases across teams ensure that integrated capabilities are available together rather than in incompatible partial states.
Shared backlog visibility:
Programme-level backlog visibility enables investment decisions and priority trade-offs across teams rather than within each team in isolation.
Reduced duplication:
Programme-level visibility of what each team is building prevents the common failure mode of multiple teams building overlapping capabilities independently.
Governance integration:
The regular cadence of PI boundaries, System Demos, and Inspect and Adapt events provides natural insertion points for governance and compliance activities.
Predictable planning horizon:
The PI cadence gives business stakeholders a predictable planning horizon for committing to milestone dates and communicating with customers.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Framework overhead:
SAFe in particular adds significant process and role overhead that can slow down small or early-stage programmes where the coordination problems it solves do not yet exist.
Exploration incompatibility:
Sprint-based planning assumes deliverable work; research spikes and AI experiments are harder to plan and estimate within standard sprint structures.
Certification dependency:
Effective SAFe implementation typically requires practitioners with specific certifications or experience; organisations that implement it without this expertise often see limited benefit.
Role proliferation:
SAFe introduces roles—Release Train Engineer, System Architect, Business Owners—that may duplicate or conflict with existing programme management roles.
Cultural resistance:
Large organisations with established project management cultures often resist the team autonomy and decentralised decision-making that scaled agile frameworks require to work effectively.
How It Works

In a SAFe implementation for an agentic AI programme, multiple teams are organised into an Agile Release Train focused on a shared business objective—for example, deploying a suite of customer service agents across multiple channels. The ART operates on a twelve-week PI cadence. At the start of each PI, all teams in the ART gather for a two-day PI Planning event where they plan their work for the next twelve weeks, identify cross-team dependencies, and commit to PI Objectives.

During the PI, teams operate in two-week sprints with standard Scrum ceremonies. At the end of each sprint, the ART runs a System Demo where all teams demonstrate the integrated capabilities built in that sprint. This integration point is more frequent than traditional integration milestones and helps surface incompatibilities while they are still small. At the end of the PI, an Inspect and Adapt workshop reviews the PI results against the PI Objectives, identifies process improvements, and incorporates learnings into the next PI planning.

For agentic AI work, the ART backlog includes both feature work—new agent capabilities—and research work—experiments, evaluation improvements, model upgrades—organised into separate backlog categories with different planning and estimation approaches. Research work may be time-boxed rather than story-pointed, with the team committing to spend a defined number of sprint days on the experiment and producing a decision rather than a specific deliverable.

In a LeSS implementation, the coordination overhead is lighter. Multiple Scrum teams share a single Product Backlog and synchronise through joint Sprint Planning events where all teams plan the upcoming sprint together, making cross-team dependencies visible before they become blockers. The Product Owner in a LeSS implementation has a broader scope than in single-team Scrum, requiring the ability to prioritise across multiple teams with competing resource demands.
Architecture

The scaled agile framework operates across five layers. At the team layer, individual Scrum teams operate with standard agile ceremonies adapted for AI work—including research spike backlog categories and model performance metrics in their definition of done. Multiple teams coordinate at the ART or LeSS team layer through shared Sprint Planning, dependency boards, and Scrum of Scrums. The programme sync layer—the PI Planning event or the LeSS cross-team Sprint events—aligns all teams on shared objectives and surfaced dependencies. The portfolio layer manages investment priorities and stage gates above the programme level. The AI governance layer integrates compliance, safety, and ethical AI reviews into the delivery cadence at defined checkpoints.
When to Use It
Four or more AI development teams:
Coordination benefits of scaled frameworks typically outweigh overhead for programmes with four or more teams with cross-team dependencies.
Shared platform dependencies:
When multiple agent development teams share model serving, evaluation, or data infrastructure, a structured coordination framework prevents platform bottlenecks.
Hard integration milestones:
When regulatory or business requirements create hard deadlines for integrated capabilities, the synchronised release cadence of scaled frameworks provides alignment.
Existing SAFe adoption:
Organisations already using SAFe for software development can extend the existing ART structure to include AI teams with adaptations for AI-specific work types.
How to Implement
Assess fit:
Evaluate whether the coordination problems of your programme justify the overhead of a full scaled agile framework, or whether a lighter coordination approach is sufficient.
Choose and adapt the framework:
Select SAFe, LeSS, or Scrum@Scale based on your organisational context and the complexity of your programme. Adapt the framework for AI-specific work types before implementing.
Define the AI-specific backlog categories:
Create explicit backlog categories for research work, model experiments, and evaluation tasks with appropriate planning and estimation approaches.
Configure the ART or team structure:
Organise teams into the selected framework's coordination structure, including platform teams and feature teams, with clear ownership boundaries.
Design the PI Planning process for AI work:
Adapt the PI Planning event to accommodate AI-specific dependencies—model training timelines, evaluation requirements—that differ from standard software dependencies.
Integrate governance checkpoints:
Map compliance reviews, security assessments, and AI safety evaluations to specific points in the PI cadence where they can be completed without disrupting sprint execution.
Train the ART:
Ensure all teams and key roles—RTE, Product Management, System Architect—understand both the framework and the AI-specific adaptations before beginning the first PI.
Examples

# pi-planning-template.yaml — SAFe PI Planning adapted for agentic AI programme
pi_planning_agenda:
  day_1:
    - time: "08:00-09:00"
      activity: "Business context and vision"
      facilitator: "Business Owner"
      ai_adaptation: "Include model performance trends and AI capability roadmap alongside business metrics"
    - time: "09:00-10:30"
      activity: "Architecture vision and technical guardrails"
      facilitator: "System Architect"
      ai_adaptation: "Cover: model serving platform capabilities, evaluation harness updates, data access controls, AI safety requirements for the PI"
    - time: "10:30-12:00"
      activity: "Programme backlog and priorities"
      facilitator: "Product Management"
      ai_adaptation: "Separate features from research spikes; time-box research work; present AI-specific epics alongside standard features"
    - time: "13:00-16:00"
      activity: "Team breakouts (sprint planning)"
      facilitator: "Each team's Scrum Master"
      ai_adaptation: "Include model training timelines as explicit sprint constraints; identify evaluation dependencies"
    - time: "16:00-17:00"
      activity: "Draft plan review and dependency mapping"
      facilitator: "RTE"
      ai_adaptation: "Programme board includes model inference dependencies and evaluation pipeline dependencies alongside standard story dependencies"
  day_2:
    - time: "08:00-10:00"
      activity: "Finalise plans and resolve dependencies"
      facilitator: "RTE"
    - time: "10:00-11:00"
      activity: "Risk identification and ROAM"
      facilitator: "RTE"
      ai_adaptation: "ROAM categories include model accuracy risk, data quality risk, and safety evaluation risk"
    - time: "11:00-12:00"
      activity: "PI Objectives commitment"
      facilitator: "Each team"
      ai_adaptation: "PI Objectives include SMART model performance targets alongside feature delivery commitments"
    - time: "13:00-14:00"
      activity: "System Demo readiness and confidence vote"
      facilitator: "RTE"

pi_objectives_template:
  format: "SMART: specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-boxed to PI"
  categories:
    - feature_delivery: "Deploy X capability to Y users by PI end"
    - model_performance: "Achieve Z% accuracy on evaluation harness by PI end"
    - platform_capability: "Deploy model serving upgrade supporting W requests/second by sprint 4"
    - research_decision: "Complete experiment and make build/buy/defer decision on V approach by sprint 2"
Real-World Example

A large telecommunications company with five agentic AI development teams and two platform teams adopted a SAFe-inspired ART structure after experiencing repeated integration failures due to undiscovered cross-team dependencies. Before SAFe adoption, teams were running independent sprints with monthly programme syncs; integration testing happened at the end of each quarter and regularly uncovered incompatibilities that required two to four weeks of unplanned remediation.

After implementing a twelve-week PI cadence with a two-day PI Planning event at the start of each PI, the first planning session surfaced twenty-three previously unknown cross-team dependencies. Seventeen were resolved during the planning event; six required plan adjustments that delayed three features by one sprint each—a significantly lower cost than discovering the same issues at the end-of-quarter integration. System Demos at the end of each two-week sprint meant that integration problems were caught within fourteen days rather than twelve weeks. The programme reduced unplanned integration remediation time by sixty-two percent in the first two PIs after adoption.
Common Mistakes
Full SAFe without adaptation for AI:
Applying SAFe verbatim to AI programmes forces AI-specific work into story-point structures that do not fit, reducing team commitment reliability.
PI Planning without architecture vision:
PI Planning events that do not include a strong architecture vision session produce plans that are locally coherent but architecturally inconsistent.
System Demos without integration:
System Demos that show individual team outputs rather than integrated capabilities miss the primary value of the regular demo cadence.
Treating the RTE as a project manager:

The Release Train Engineer role is a servant leader, not a traditional project manager; organisations that hire PMs into the RTE role often see the ART operate as a waterfall project within a SAFe wrapper.
Skipping the Inspect and Adapt workshop:
The end-of-PI retrospective is where the ART improves its process; skipping it means the programme does not improve across PIs.
Best Practices
Start with LeSS before SAFe:
For programmes with fewer than eight teams, LeSS provides sufficient coordination with significantly less overhead than full SAFe.
Create explicit AI backlog categories:
Research spikes, model experiments, and evaluation tasks should be distinct backlog items with different planning approaches from standard features.
Run tight System Demos:
System Demos should demonstrate integrated capabilities, not individual team outputs, and should be completed in under ninety minutes to maintain stakeholder attendance.
Invest in the RTE role:
The Release Train Engineer is the most leveraged role in the ART; underinvesting in this role disproportionately undermines the programme's coordination effectiveness.
ROAM AI-specific risks:
Extend the ROAM (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) risk categorisation process to explicitly address AI-specific risks—model accuracy, data quality, safety.
Performance and Scalability

SAFe scales to very large AI programmes—twenty or more teams—through the addition of a Solution Train layer above the ART layer. For most agentic AI programmes, a single ART of four to ten teams is sufficient. As programmes grow beyond a single ART, the inter-ART coordination mechanisms—Solution Demo, Solution Train sync—must be explicitly designed and resourced. The primary scaling risk in large SAFe implementations is that the coordination overhead grows faster than the coordination benefit, creating diminishing returns on scale.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Jira Advanced Roadmaps:
SAFe and Scrum at scale tracking with PI Planning boards, team-level sprint tracking, and programme-level dependency visualisation.
Miro or MURAL:
Virtual PI Planning facilitation tools for distributed ART teams, with programme board templates and dependency mapping.
Atlassian Confluence:
Documentation of PI Objectives, team norms, architecture decisions, and inspection and adaptation retrospective outputs.
Scaled Agile tooling (Rally, Targetprocess):
Purpose-built SAFe implementation tools with native PI Planning, ART Kanban, and programme metrics.
Comparison with Alternatives
Independent Scrum teams with monthly syncs:
Minimal overhead but insufficient coordination for programmes with significant cross-team dependencies; integration failures are discovered late.
Traditional programme management:
Provides coordination but without the team autonomy that is essential for productive AI development; often produces bottlenecks at the programme manager.
SAFe for large programmes (5+ teams):
Best for programmes with complex dependencies and hard release boundaries; highest overhead but provides the strongest coordination and governance integration.
LeSS for medium programmes (2-4 teams):
Lower overhead than SAFe with strong coordination; best for programmes without hard release synchronisation requirements.
Security and Reliability

Scaled agile frameworks for agentic AI programmes should integrate security reviews and AI safety evaluations as first-class programme board items rather than treating them as external compliance activities that happen outside the sprint cadence. In SAFe, the Enabler Epic category is appropriate for security and safety work that does not produce direct user-visible features but enables the programme to maintain its compliance obligations. Tracking these enabler items on the programme board makes the security and safety work visible to all ART stakeholders and prevents it from being consistently deprioritised in favour of feature delivery.
Key Takeaways
Scaled agile frameworks provide the coordination structures—shared planning, dependency boards, synchronised release cadences—that prevent multi-team AI programmes from becoming incoherent.
SAFe works best for large programmes with hard release boundaries; LeSS is a lighter-weight option for smaller programmes with less need for tight release synchronisation.
AI-specific adaptations are necessary: research spikes and model experiments need distinct backlog categories with time-box-based planning rather than story-point estimation.
PI Planning events for AI programmes must include an architecture vision that covers model serving, evaluation, and data platform capabilities alongside feature delivery.
System Demos must demonstrate integrated capabilities—not individual team outputs—to provide genuine integration assurance at the sprint boundary.
The Release Train Engineer role is the most leveraged investment in a SAFe ART; the quality of this role significantly determines the coordination effectiveness of the programme.
Adapting Sprint Ceremonies for AI-Specific Work

The standard Scrum ceremonies—sprint planning, daily standup, sprint review, and sprint retrospective—require adaptation when applied to agentic AI development teams because the nature of AI work differs from conventional software development in ways that affect how these ceremonies should be structured and what questions they should address.

Sprint planning for AI teams must accommodate the uncertainty in task duration that is inherent to model experimentation. A conventional software story can be reliably estimated to a sprint size because the implementation approach is clear before planning begins. A research spike—validating whether a new embedding model improves retrieval accuracy—has an uncertain outcome and uncertain duration. Sprint planning for AI teams should time-box research spikes rather than estimating them in story points, allocating a fixed number of sprint days for the experiment and defining the output as a written decision document rather than a working feature.

The daily standup for AI teams should expand beyond the standard three questions—what did I do yesterday, what will I do today, is anything blocking me—to include a brief signal on experiment trajectories. A daily standup question of 'are your experiments tracking towards the expected result, or are they showing surprising results that need team discussion' surfaces AI-specific coordination needs that the standard questions miss. This adaptation does not need to lengthen the standup significantly—a brief yes-or-no signal with an offer to discuss offline is sufficient.

Sprint reviews for AI teams should include a model performance section alongside the feature demonstration section. Showing the trend of the primary evaluation metric over the sprint—even if no new features were deployed—communicates the health of the AI development trajectory to stakeholders. A sprint that deployed significant feature work but saw model accuracy decline needs to explain the accuracy decline in the sprint review; hiding it in favour of only showing the feature work misleads stakeholders about the actual programme health.
Time-box research spikes:
Allocate a fixed number of sprint days and define the output as a decision document, not a working feature.
Daily signal on experiment trajectory:
Add a brief daily signal question about whether experiments are tracking as expected or showing surprising results requiring team discussion.
Include model metrics in sprint reviews:
Show evaluation metric trends alongside feature demonstrations to give stakeholders a complete picture of AI programme health.
Track spike completion rates:
A team that consistently fails to complete research spikes within their time box has an estimation or scope problem that needs retrospective attention.
Governance Integration at ART Level

Agentic AI programmes operating in regulated industries or with significant safety implications must integrate compliance, safety, and ethical AI reviews into the ART delivery cadence. Without explicit integration, governance reviews become events that happen outside the sprint cadence—typically at the end of a development phase—where they can only raise problems that are expensive to fix at that stage. Integrating governance into the ART cadence moves these reviews to the point where issues can still be addressed with low remediation cost.

The most effective governance integration pattern is the definition of governance checkpoints as ART-level events within the PI plan. Rather than treating a compliance review as an external dependency, the PI plan should include a sprint in which a compliance review milestone is a PI Objective item. The teams contributing to the compliance review produce the evidence package as sprint work, and the compliance review outcome is reported at the System Demo. This treats governance as a first-class programme concern rather than an overhead activity external to the delivery cadence.

Safety evaluation deserves special treatment in the ART cadence for agentic AI programmes. Automated safety evaluation—running the agent through a battery of adversarial prompts and failure mode scenarios—should be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline so that every model update triggers an immediate safety evaluation. The results of these automated evaluations should be reported at the ART Sync as a programme health signal. Human review—where domain experts assess agent outputs for subtle safety concerns that automated evaluation misses—should be scheduled as a sprint ceremony at defined intervals, typically once per PI for high-risk agent capabilities.
Include governance in PI Objectives:
Treat compliance reviews, security assessments, and safety evaluations as PI Objective items rather than external activities.
Automate safety evaluation in CI/CD:
Every model update should trigger an automated safety evaluation with results reported in the ART Sync.
Schedule human safety reviews as sprint ceremonies:
At least once per PI, domain experts review agent outputs for safety concerns that automated evaluation does not detect.
Report governance health at System Demo:
Include a governance status section in every System Demo to maintain visibility of compliance and safety status alongside feature delivery.
